rationale: Naylor et al. is cited in the hypothesis-development section to justify a core prediction about when the uptrend effect should weaken, not just as generic background. The paper returns to this citation when discussing Study 4 findings, explicitly stating consistency with egocentric anchoring work. Although cited alongside other sources, the role is clear and tied to conceptual setup and interpretation of results. This is therefore a meaningful application of the cited paper's mechanism in a new messaging context.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Naylor et al. (2011) as a theoretical basis for its similarity boundary condition (H3), arguing that people discount behavioral information from dissimilar others due to egocentric anchoring and false-consensus processes. It then applies that logic to uptrend health messaging and interprets Study 4 attenuation results through this lens.
